mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the scientific study of human behaviour and experience across cognitive, developmental, and social dimensions (A.3, B).
Directly addresses the evolutionary development of mankind and phylogenetic continuity with other primates (A.1.c).
Covers natural selection and evolutionary theory underlying the explanation of distinctive human traits via phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c).
Covers behavioural capacities humans share with other primates and higher mammals, including instinct and learning (A.1.a).
Covers mammalian behaviour, brain, and evolution, directly relevant to capacities humans share with higher mammals (A.1.a).
Covers elements of genetic endowment including DNA, heredity, and variation central to the nature-nurture controversy (A.2.a).
Directly addresses heredity, genome, and variation as the genetic side of the nature-nurture controversy (A.2.a).
Covers cultural conditioning and socialization as environmental factors shaping behaviour, and cumulative transmission of culture (A.1.b, A.2.b).
Covers ecological factors as environmental conditions influencing organisms and their behaviour (A.2.b).
Directly covers sensation and perception as the cognitive dimension of behaviour and experience (A.3.a).
Directly covers memory and its role in the cognitive dimension of behaviour (A.3.a).
Covers vision and pattern recognition, relevant to the cognitive dimension of sensation and perception (A.3.a).
Directly covers concept formation, ideation, and reasoning as cognitive dimensions of behaviour (A.3.a).
Covers reasoning, problem solving, and rational thinking as cognitive capacities (A.3.a).
Covers the neural foundations of cognition including sensation, perception, memory, and reasoning (A.3.a).
Covers the neural substrate underlying cognitive, conative, and affective dimensions of behaviour (A.3).
Directly addresses the voluntary and involuntary, self-determination, and motivation as the conative dimension (A.3.b).
Directly covers emotions, empathy, and the affective dimension of behaviour (A.3.c).
Covers the history of how emotions, feelings, and affect have been understood, relevant to the affective dimension (A.3.c).
Covers the pleasant and unpleasant as part of the affective dimension of experience, including personality and well-being (A.3.c).
Covers cognitive abilities, IQ, and working memory, relevant to the nature-nurture controversy and cognitive capacities (A.2, A.3.a).
Addresses conscious experience, self-awareness, and the nature of human behavioural capacities held distinctive of humans (A.1.b).
Addresses the mind-body relationship, consciousness, sensation, and perception as foundational to human experience.
Addresses whether mankind is distinct from or continuous with nature, and the nature-nurture debate (A.1, A.2).
Propositional language is identified as a distinctively human behavioural capacity (A.1.b).
Covers language structure and speech, relevant to propositional language as a distinctive human trait (A.1.b).
Directly addresses the comparison of human and artificial, machine-created intelligence (A.5).
Covers prenatal growth and development, cell differentiation, and nervous system formation (B.1, B.2).
Covers cognitive development, learning, and brain development in infancy and childhood (B.4, B.5).
Directly covers the adolescence stage from puberty to adulthood (B.6).
Addresses self-identity, agency, and subjectivity as aspects of human experience across developmental stages.
Covers creative expression as a behavioural capacity held to be distinctive of humans (A.1.b).
Covers biological rhythms as elements of genetic endowment shaping physiological characteristics (A.2.a).
